PREFACE 


ee 


The third chapter of the present work, the commentary on the lokanirdeéa 
of Abhidharmakoga-bhasya was already edited by Prof. L. de La Vallée Poussin 
between the years 1913 and 1914 p. Chr. and appeared in book form; the first chapter 
of the same was also edited by Prof. 8. Lévi and became visible: in the Bibliotheca 
Buddhica Vol. XXI. 1918. The editorial task of the rest of this commentary in 
the same serics had been entrusted to me. For this purpose, my manuscript had 
been completed long ago. But influenced by either the Great War or the 
demolition of the printing factory by an unexpected inundation or other hinde- 
rances, the printing works made little progress. In 19381, only a part of its 
second chapter was published by me. Such being the circumstances, I was very 
anxious that it might be impossible to publish the whole volume in my life time. 

However, in the seventh year of Showa (1982 p. Chr.), one association called 
“the Publishing Association of Abhidharmako§ga-vyakhya&”’ which aimed at 
publishing the whole work in Roman letters, was organized by the origination 
of Mr. E. Tomomatsu with the approbation of many competent fellowscholors. 
Encouraged by this association, with all kinds of hardships of more than three 
years, I accomplished this work with difficulty. As the principal original, the 
Calcutta-MS. (C) was used and collated with that of the Bibliothéque Nationale 
in Paris, copicd in tracing it by the Late Dr. B. Nanjio (N). To begin with, 
two more other MSS., that is, one brought home by Dr. J. Takakusu and kept 
in the Tokyo Impcrial University (T). The other brought home by Dr. R. 
Sakaki and stored in the Kyoto Imperial University (K), were consulted for 
reference. But, during the process, I was obliged to give up these last two 
because they contained many errata and made no contribution at all for emenda- 
tion. The first and third chapters of the present work have been compared 
with those of Prof. §. Lévi’s (L) and Prof. L. de La Vallée Poussin’s (P) res- 
pectively and its effects have sometimes been put in the foot-note. 

In regard to the Samdhi, it is not regularly followed as the case common 
with all other Buddhist MSS. Moreover, the punctuation marks are frequently 
so confusedly used that they have to be arranged in their proper positions from 
the meaning of the sentence; and also only at the end of sentences which are 


punctuated, all of the Samdhi which require to be corrected are changed in the 
regular form. But, those of the rest should be kept in mind by readers as the 
characteristics of the Buddhist MSS. as a whole, and that they are so left 
untouched, because when one tries to make any correction of the mistakes in the 
manuscript they should give him an important foundation or suggestion. The 
Italics in the present work are generally used to denote the words in the original 
text commented by YaSomitra; and sometimes, the qoutations which were taken 
Jrom other parts of the same text which ought to be written in Roman characters 
as a rule. 

The original text by Vasubandhu has not yet been discovered except some 
of the Karikaés. However, the Chinese version of the same text by Paramartha 
is faithfully rendered word for word, and the fact that the arrangement of terms 
closely pursues that of the original might be inferred from the sentence of the 
present commentary. Vasubandhu’s Bhésyam of the Karikaé explains not only 
the limited number of words of one Sloka or a half-sloka or one line, but even 
two or three kariké-words whenever the case requires. 

On the other hand, the order of words in the Chinese version by Hiuen- 
Thsang, though exquisite in terminology, does not agree with that of the original 
text. Hiuen-Thsang classified the Karika-sentences and their respective Bhasyam 
in a more comprehensive way than that of the original so as to explain at least 
one whole line at a time. Though the divisional method of paragraphs in the 
Tibetan version differs from that of Hiuen-Thsang, the principle of its classifica- 
tion is similar to his. Therefore, readers may realize that the position of a term 
or a passage in the present commentary does not always coincide with these two 
of Hiuen-Thsang’s and the Tibetan version. So that, in order to understand the 
meanings laid down in this commentary, if a reader, taking Paramétha’s version 
as the original text, pursue his study, he would certainly feel himself as if he 
read the real Sanscrit original. Nevertheless, the numeration of Karikas, for 
convenience’s sake, does not thoroughly depend upon Paramdartha’s, but on 
Hiuen-Thsang. 

The Tibetan version is, I might say, absolutely necessary for reference to 
make up the deficiencies of letters or to revise the clerical errors or to com- 
prehend cleary about the dubious and doubtful passages, in the course of study. 
Though it is so, in Tokyo, unhappily, we have only one of the Gnas-brtan edition 
which is so dubious in print that no one can read this smoothly. Accordingly, 


if we desire to complete this study, we must seek other editions. So, I, at first, 
borrowed the Peking edition deposited now in the Otani University, Kyoto, from 
Mr. Enga Teramoto to whom my Sincere thanks should be paid for the kind- 
ness shown me by him, and examined several parts of my work with this; 
secondly, Mr. Tokwan Tada, a lecturer at the Tohoku Imperial University, who 
was then not yet acquainted with me, accommodated me with the parts of the 
chapters I—III, of his Sde-dge edition; but, I was very sorry because I, despite 
his former promise, could not borrow the rest of that edition from him. How- 
ever sorry I may be, I have to express my sincere acknowledgment to him. 
Under such circumstances as stated above, it was inevitable but that I should 
resort to the Gnas-brtan edition possessed by Mr. Ekai Kawaguchi who willingly 
lent me two copies of that edition and enabled me to compare with them. from 
the fourth to the last chapters and to give finally their leaf-numbers. If I could 
not borrow this edition, most parts of the present work, without comparing with 
the Tibetan version, might have not appeared in public. So, I am now keenly 
aware of the unbiased magnanimity shown me by him and desire him to accept 


my cordial thanks. 
October, 10th year of Showa. 


U. WOGIHARA.
